abstract: This chapter provides a comprehensive evaluation of the global water cycle, synthesizing multiple lines of evidence to track its past, present, and projected future states. It builds upon foundational findings regarding climate extremes and regional change, incorporating a wide array of data sources—including paleoclimate reconstructions, satellite observations, atmospheric reanalyses, and the latest generation of global and regional model simulations. The assessment highlights the physical drivers behind water cycle shifts, offers a formal attribution of observed changes to human influence, and explores future projections alongside their inherent uncertainties and the risk of abrupt transitions. Our findings reveal that human activities have already caused widespread and non-uniform alterations to the water cycle. While these shifts were frequently obscured during the 20th century by the competing effects of different climatic drivers, such as aerosol cooling, the global water cycle will be increasingly and more clearly dominated by greenhouse gas forcing as the 21st century progresses.
